heading: The Surprise Nobody
Wanted
warmup.intro: Last class you chased a colleague, and you closed like this:

callback.text: Could you get it to me by Thursday?
callback.speak: Could you get it to me by Thursday?

warmup.box_title: Every one of those fights starts on day one, in silence.
warmup.box_text: Today is the kickoff of Project Atlas. Nobody has said what is NOT included. In four months, that silence becomes a fight. Say it now.

goal.d: Eight words to draw the line
Three role-plays, and she assumes everything

heading: The Language of the
Kickoff
vocab_ch.sub: 8 words to say what is in, and what is not

vocab.def: To make sure everybody has the same picture

vocab.hint: A concrete thing you hand over

This has to be a mutual agreement, not my decision.

heading: The Fight That Started on Day
One
vocab_check.prompt: Think of a project at Oracle that ended in a fight about scope.
vocab_check.sub: Talk for 90 seconds: what did the client assume, what did nobody say at the kickoff, and what did it cost?

reading.ch_sub: A short text about the fight that begins in silence
heading: The Fight Starts on Day
One
reading.rtitle: The Fight Starts on Day One
paras: Almost every fight at the end of a project was born at the beginning, in silence. The client assumed the reports were included. Nobody said they were not. Four months later, two teams are angry, and both of them are right.

The cure is uncomfortable and it takes ten minutes. On day one, say what is in scope -- and then, out loud, say what is out of scope. 'We will deliver the migration and the training. We will not deliver the custom reports in this phase.' People do not get angry about limits. They get angry about surprises.

Then agree on two or three ground rules, and on who signs off. Not five people: one. Write it on one page, send it, and ask for a yes in writing. It feels bureaucratic on day one. On day one hundred, it is the reason you still have a client.

choices: Say what is in scope and what is OUT of scope on day one, and get it in writing.
Avoid talking about limits at the start, so the client stays happy.
Scope fights are impossible to prevent.

tf: People get angry about limits, not about surprises.
It is the opposite: limits are fine, surprises are not.
You should say what is OUT of scope, out loud, on day one.
'We will not deliver the custom reports in this phase.'
One person should sign off, not five.
One decision-maker, agreed at the kickoff.

heading: How to Draw the
Line
bank.sub: In scope → out of scope → the ground rules → who signs off → the yes in writing
bank.label: The five lines of a kickoff
items: Let me be upfront about what is in and what is out.
In scope: the migration, the training and two reports.
Out of scope in this phase: the custom reports and the mobile app.
Two ground rules: no new requests after Friday, and one decision-maker.
Who signs off on the design -- you, or your director?
Can we agree on that, and can I send it to you in writing today?

grammar.prompt: Which one includes? Which one excludes? Which one closes the agreement?

discovery.html: "The migration and the training are in scope."
discovery.speak: The migration and the training are in scope.

discovery.html: "The custom reports are out of scope in this phase."
discovery.speak: The custom reports are out of scope in this phase.

discovery.html: "We will deliver the migration by June 20th. We will not deliver the mobile app."
discovery.speak: We will deliver the migration by June 20th. We will not deliver the mobile app.

discovery.html: "Can we agree on two ground rules?"
discovery.speak: Can we agree on two ground rules?

rows: Y is / are out of scope + we will not + verb
The reports are out of scope. We will not deliver them in June.
Can we agree on + noun? / Let us agree on...
Can we agree on two ground rules?
grammar.note: Silence is not politeness -- it is a bill you pay later. Saying what is OUT of scope on day one costs you ten uncomfortable minutes. Not saying it costs you the client, four months later.
mistakes: We will delivery the reports in June.
We will deliver the reports in June.
Let us to agree the ground rules.
Let us agree on the ground rules.
grammar.mistake_note: Deliver é o verbo; delivery é o substantivo (a entrega). E depois de let us vem o verbo puro: "let us agree on" -- nunca "let us to agree".

heading: The Kickoff
One-Pager
artefact.title: What Is In. What Is Out.
artefact.reason: In scope: the migration, the training, and two standard reports
rows: Out of scope in this phase: the custom reports and the mobile app
Ground rule 1: no new requests after Friday of each sprint
Ground rule 2: one decision-maker on your side -- not a committee
Sign-off: your director approves the design by June 5th, in writing
artefact.footer: One page, sent the same day, with a yes in writing. Ten uncomfortable minutes now, or four angry months later.

heading: Say the Uncomfortable Part
Today
listenings.text: Diogo, the kickoff is at four, and I want one thing from you. Say the uncomfortable part out loud. Every project we lose, we lose because nobody said no at the beginning. The client will assume the custom reports are included. They are not. If you do not say it today, in front of everybody, then in September it becomes a conversation about money, and you will lose it. Say what is in, say what is out, and agree on who signs off. One person, not a committee. And put it on one page and send it the same day. If they say yes in writing, you have a project. If they do not, you have a problem -- and it is better to have it today.
comp: Why do projects get lost, according to Sophia?
Because nobody says no at the beginning.
That the custom reports are included.
What does she want on the page, and when?
What is in, what is out, and who signs off -- sent the same day.

heading: The Kickoff,
Done Well
listenings.lead: This is the target. Listen, then do it your way.
listenings.text: Before we start the work, let me be upfront about the boundaries, because I would rather have this conversation today than in September. In scope for this phase: the migration, the training for your team, and two standard reports. Out of scope in this phase: the custom reports and the mobile app. They are not cancelled -- they are simply not in this phase, and we can plan them for the autumn. Two ground rules, if you agree. First: no new requests after Friday of each sprint, because a change on a Tuesday costs us a week. Second: one decision-maker on your side. Not a committee -- one person who signs off on the design by June 5th. Can we agree on that? I will send it in one page today, and I would like a yes in writing before we start.
comp: What is out of scope in this phase?
The custom reports and the mobile app.
What are the two ground rules?
No new requests after Friday, and one decision-maker who signs off.
What does he want before the work starts?
A yes in writing, on the one-page summary.

heading: I Assumed That Was
Included

lines.html: Before we start, let me be upfront about what is in scope and what is not.
lines.speak: Before we start, let me be upfront about what is in scope and what is not.

lines.html: We know the scope. Migration, training, reports. Let us move on.
lines.speak: We know the scope. Migration, training, reports. Let us move on.

lines.html: Two standard reports are in. The custom reports are out of scope in this phase.
lines.speak: Two standard reports are in. The custom reports are out of scope in this phase.

lines.html: Wait. I assumed the custom reports were included. The client expects them.
lines.speak: Wait. I assumed the custom reports were included. The client expects them.

lines.html: That is exactly why I am saying it now, and not in September. They are not cancelled -- we plan them for the autumn.
lines.speak: That is exactly why I am saying it now, and not in September. They are not cancelled. We plan them for the autumn.

lines.html: Fine. What else are you taking away from me?
lines.speak: Fine. What else are you taking away from me?

lines.html: Nothing. But I want two ground rules: no new requests after Friday, and one person who signs off on the design.
lines.speak: Nothing. But I want two ground rules: no new requests after Friday, and one person who signs off on the design.

lines.html: The client has a committee. They all want to decide.
lines.speak: The client has a committee. They all want to decide.

lines.html: Then the committee chooses one name. Ownership has to be with one person. Can we align on that today, in writing?
lines.speak: Then the committee chooses one name. Ownership has to be with one person. Can we align on that today, in writing?

comp: That the custom reports were included in this phase.
What does Diogo say about the custom reports?
They are not cancelled -- they are planned for the autumn.
What does he ask for, about the client's committee?
That the committee chooses one name -- one person with ownership.

quickfire.situation: Open the kickoff with the uncomfortable part.
tips: 'Let me be upfront about what is in and what is out.'
Say it before the work, not after.

quickfire.situation: List what is in scope -- three things, no more.
Say what is OUT of scope, out loud.
tips: 'The custom reports are out of scope in this phase.'

quickfire.situation: She says: 'But the client expects that.' Answer.
tips: That is exactly why you are saying it today.
Offer a phase, not a no.
Give the reason: a change on Tuesday costs a week.

quickfire.situation: Close: who signs off, and by when?
tips: Ask for the yes in writing.

items: Are you saying you will not help us if we need something extra?
Why do you need this in writing? Do you not trust us?
The committee will never accept one decision-maker. Now what?
pressure.followup: In first, out second, rules third. Never say a flat no -- say "not in this phase", and give the phase.

heading: Run the
Kickoff
guided.prompt: Ten minutes. Open Project Atlas and draw the line.
guided.sub: Use the kickoff one-pager. Five moves, in this order:
chips: out of scope in this phase: ...

heading: She Assumes
Everything
semi.quote: We assumed all the reports were included. That was always the deal.
semi.sub: She pushes twice: first she says the reports were always included, then she refuses to name one decision-maker. Do not fight about the past. Say what is in, what is out, offer a phase for the rest, and do not close without one name.
chips: that is why I am saying it today
not cancelled -- not in this phase
the ground rule protects both of us

heading: Your Real
Kickoff
free.headline: Two minutes. No notes. One line in the sand.
free.sub: Take a real project at Oracle that is starting -- or one that went wrong. Run the kickoff out loud: in scope, out of scope, two ground rules, and who signs off, by when.

survival.en: Let me be upfront about what is in and what is out.
survival.pt: Deixa eu ser direto sobre o que está dentro e o que está fora.

survival.en: The custom reports are out of scope in this phase.
survival.pt: Os relatórios customizados estão fora do escopo desta fase.

survival.en: They are not cancelled -- we plan them for the autumn.
survival.pt: Eles não estão cancelados: planejamos para o outono.

survival.en: Two ground rules: no new requests after Friday, and one decision-maker.
survival.pt: Duas regras básicas: nada novo depois de sexta, e um único decisor.

survival.en: Can we agree on that today, in writing?
survival.pt: Podemos fechar isso hoje, por escrito?

checks: I say what is in scope, and what is out -- on day one.
I can say "not in this phase" instead of a flat no.
I propose ground rules, with the reason behind them.
I make sure one person signs off, not a committee.
I get the agreement in writing, the same day.

closing.line: Ten uncomfortable minutes today, instead of four angry months in September.
closing.next_desc: The two minutes of small talk before the call really starts

order_audio.text: Diogo opens the kickoff and says he wants to be upfront about the scope. He lists what is in: the migration, the training and two standard reports. Then he says what is out of scope in this phase: the custom reports. Sophia says she assumed they were included. Finally, Diogo proposes two ground rules and asks who signs off on the design.
items: He lists what is in scope: migration, training, two reports.
Sophia says she assumed the custom reports were included.
Diogo opens the kickoff and says he will be upfront about scope.
Diogo proposes two ground rules and asks who signs off.
He says the custom reports are out of scope in this phase.

preclass.desc: Draw the line on day one: what is in scope, what is out, the ground rules, and who signs off.
preclass.context: Every scope fight is born in silence, on day one. So Diogo is upfront: "Let me align with you on what is in and what is out." In scope: the migration, the training, and two standard deliverables. Out of scope in this phase: the custom reports. Sophia assumed they were included -- and that assumption, in silence, would have become a fight in September. He does not say a flat no. He says "not in this phase", and gives the phase. Then he proposes two ground rules: no new requests after Friday, and one decision-maker. The client has a committee, but ownership has to be with one person -- one name, one person who signs off on the design. Clear boundaries are not aggression: they protect both sides. And the agreement has to be mutual, in writing, the same day.
quiz_ctx: Why does Diogo say what is OUT of scope on day one?
Because a silent assumption becomes a fight four months later.
Because he does not want to do the extra work.
Because the client asked him to.
We will deliver the reports in June.
We will delivery the reports in June.
We will to deliver the reports in June.
What does he ask about the sign-off?
That one person, not a committee, approves the design.
That the whole committee approves everything.
That nobody has to approve anything.
preclass.tip_sub: Alinhar expectativa no dia um: o que entra, o que NÃO entra, e quem aprova. / Setting expectations on day one.
tip_rows: Y is / are out of scope + we will not + verbo
The reports are out of scope in this phase.
Can we agree on + substantivo? / Let us agree on...
Can we agree on two ground rules?
preclass.tip_key: Deliver = verbo (entregar); delivery = substantivo (a entrega). Depois de let us vem o verbo puro ("let us agree on"). E nunca diga um não seco: diga not in this phase, e dê a fase.

blanks.hint: Hint: at the beginning, before the work / desde o início
blanks.phrase: Let me be upfront about what is in and what is out.
blanks.post: about what is in and what is out."

blanks.hint: Hint: out of ___ = fora do escopo
blanks.phrase: The custom reports are out of scope in this phase.

blanks.hint: Hint: the VERB (deliver), not the noun (delivery)
blanks.phrase: We will deliver the migration by June 20th.

blanks.hint: Hint: the rules of how we work together / regras básicas
blanks.phrase: Two ground rules: no new requests after Friday.

blanks.hint: Hint: approves formally, in writing / aprova formalmente
blanks.phrase: Who signs off on the design?

blanks.hint: Hint: can we ___ on that? / concordar, fechar
blanks.phrase: Can we agree on that today, in writing?

preclass.speech_intro: The five lines of a kickoff. Record each one. Friendly but firm -- limits are not aggression, they are protection.
quiz_sit: At the kickoff, the strongest opening is:
"Let me be upfront about what is in and what is out."
"We will do our best to deliver everything."
"Let us not talk about limits today."
The client assumes extra work is included. You say:
"They are not cancelled -- they are out of scope in this phase. We can plan them for the autumn."
"That was never part of the deal."
"OK, we will find a way." (and you know you cannot)
A client committee of five wants to approve everything. You:
Ask the committee to choose one name, with ownership of the sign-off.
think: Take a real project at Oracle -- one that is starting, or one that went wrong. Run the kickoff out loud, for two minutes: (1) be upfront, (2) what is in scope, (3) what is OUT of scope in this phase, (4) two ground rules, with the reason, (5) who signs off, and by when. Record it.

Now the client answers: "We assumed all of that was included. That was always the deal." Answer out loud. Do not fight about the past, and do not say a flat no -- offer a phase, and close with one decision-maker. Record it.

complementary.title: Ocean's Eleven (2001)
complementary.desc: Danny Ocean explains the plan at the start: the goal, the roles, and the rules. Everybody leaves the room with the same picture.
complementary.tip: Watch the planning scene and list how he makes each person's job clear.

complementary.title: BEP 391 -- Project Management English: Scoping a Project (1)
complementary.desc: Scoping means saying what is included and what is NOT included. This lesson shows the questions you must ask.
complementary.tip: Write 5 scoping questions in English for your next kickoff meeting.

complementary.title: How to Run an Effective Project Kickoff Meeting
complementary.desc: A full agenda for a kickoff meeting, with the parts about goals, scope and expectations.
complementary.tip: Copy the agenda and say each item out loud, as if you were leading the meeting.

teacher.title: Abertura (2 min): Compartilhe a tela. Diga: 'Last class you chased someone. Today you prevent the whole fight -- on day one, in ten uncomfortable minutes.' Avise: hoje ele fala quase o tempo todo.
teacher.warmup: Warm-up falado (4 min): Toque a frase da Aula 15. Depois a virada: 'Every fight you had was born in silence, at the kickoff.' Faca a pergunta do box e cale a boca 90 segundos.
teacher.goal: Objetivo (1 min): 'Words, a short text, and then you run a kickoff -- and I assume everything is included.' Rapido.
teacher.vocab_transition: Transicao vocab (1 min): 'Eight words. Click each card.' Ferramenta, nao aula de vocabulario.
teacher.vocab1: Vocab reveal 1-4 (4 min): Pista, tentativa, reveal, audio, uma frase real. CCQ 'align': 'Do we align before or after the work? (Before.)' CCQ 'upfront': 'Is upfront at the start or at the end? (At the start.)' CCQ 'deliverable': 'Is "good communication" a deliverable? (No -- it is not concrete.)'
teacher.vocab2: Vocab reveal 5-8 (4 min): Mesma dinamica. CCQ 'sign off': 'Is a verbal yes a sign-off? (No -- it is formal, in writing.)' CCQ 'ownership': 'Can five people own one decision? (No.)' CCQ 'boundaries': 'Do boundaries destroy relationships? (No -- they protect them.)'
teacher.vocab_check: Vocab check falado (4 min): 90 segundos sobre um projeto que terminou em briga de escopo. Minimo cinco palavras novas. Voce so escuta e conta.
teacher.ch3: Transicao leitura (1 min): 'Short text. Read it fast -- then tell me which fight of yours started exactly like this.' A leitura e trampolim.
teacher.reading: Leitura + gist (5 min): 90 segundos de leitura silenciosa. Depois o gist. E entao a pergunta que vale a aula: 'Would your client accept this on day one -- or would they call you difficult?' Dois minutos de fala dele. Discorde de proposito.
teacher.tf: True / False (3 min): Ele decide em voz alta ANTES de clicar, e justifica com uma frase do texto. Rapido.
teacher.bank: Useful language falado (4 min): Cada frase lida em voz alta e imediatamente reescrita para um projeto REAL dele. Seis frases da tela, seis frases dele.
teacher.grammar: Grammar discovery (4 min): Toque as 4 frases. Pergunta unica: 'Which one includes? Which one excludes? Which one closes the agreement?' Guie: in scope / out of scope / can we agree on.
teacher.rule: Reveal the Rule (3 min): Clique. Dentro, fora, acordado. Exija 2 frases dele com 'out of scope in this phase' sobre o projeto real. CCQ: 'Is "out of scope" the same as "cancelled"? (No.)'
teacher.mistake: Common Mistake (2 min): 'will delivery' e 'let us to agree'. Peca as versoes certas em voz alta, duas vezes.
teacher.ch5: Transicao (1 min): 'She already assumes the reports are included. They are not.' Suba a tensao.
teacher.listening1: Listening 1 (4 min): 'Sophia wants you to say the uncomfortable part -- today, in front of everybody.' Toque uma vez, sem texto.
teacher.listening1_comp: Comprehension falada (3 min): Respostas em voz alta antes do clique. Depois: 'Why is it better to have the problem TODAY?' Deixe ele deduzir falando.
teacher.artefact: Artefato (3 min): Nao leia por ele. 'Read the one-pager, then run the kickoff out loud, in five moves.' Ele fala; voce marca o que faltou.
teacher.dialogue: Dialogo (6 min): Voce e a Sophia e voce ASSUME tudo: 'I assumed the custom reports were included', 'what else are you taking away from me?'. Antes de cada fala do Diogo, ELE fala primeiro; so entao clique e toque o audio.
teacher.dialogue_comp: Dialogue comprehension (2 min): Perguntas sobre a SOPHIA. Depois: 'Was he difficult, or was he professional?' Deixe ele defender a resposta.
teacher.listening2: Listening 2 (4 min): 'This is the kickoff, done well -- 60 seconds.' Toque uma vez. Depois: 'What is out of scope? What are the two rules?' -- ele fala.
teacher.ch6: Transicao (1 min): 'From here you draw the line, and I step over it. Three times.'
teacher.quickfire: Quick Fire (5 min): Uma situacao por vez, resposta imediata. Sem correcao entre elas -- fluencia primeiro.
teacher.rp_guided: Role-play GUIADO (4 min): Voce e a cliente. Ele conduz o kickoff com os cinco movimentos do one-pager. Se ele nao disser o que esta FORA do escopo, mande refazer.
teacher.rp_semi: Role-play SEMI-LIVRE (5 min): Voce assume duas vezes: 'all the reports were always included' e depois recusa nomear um unico decisor. Ele nao pode brigar com o passado: diz o que esta dentro, o que esta fora, oferece uma fase e sai com UM nome.
teacher.rp_free: Role-play LIVRE (5 min): Sem apoio. 'A real project. Two minutes. Draw the line. Go.' NAO interrompa, cronometre e diga o tempo no fim.
teacher.pressure: Under pressure (4 min): Tres perguntas duras, uma a uma, sem aviso. Se ele der um NAO seco, pare: a resposta e 'not in this phase' + a fase.
teacher.survival: Survival Card (3 min): 'Five phrases. With these, no project ends in a surprise.' Toque cada uma; tom firme e amigavel.
teacher.learned: Wrap-up (3 min): Ele clica cada check e diz a frase que prova. So depois devolva as 2 correcoes. Homework ORAL: gravar 2 minutos de kickoff real (dentro, fora, regras, sign-off).
teacher.closing: Fechamento (1 min): Homework ORAL: gravar 2 minutos de kickoff e enviar. Anuncie a Aula 17: 'Next time: the two minutes before the call starts -- the hardest English of all.'
